mechanism_narrative: A falling federal funds rate lowers discount rates and supports equity multiples, so a negative change in FEDFUNDS predicts positive S&P 500 returns over the next month. The signal fails when policy rates are stable or rising, because no easing impulse is present.
failure_condition: If 20-day forward SPX returns are not positive in at least 55% of months when FEDFUNDS declines from the prior month, or rolling 2-year Rank IC stays below 0.03, reject the hypothesis.

mechanism_narrative: When FEDFUNDS rises above 3.0%, tighter policy increases discount rates and raises recession risk, so forward S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days should be negative. The effect should be strongest immediately after the threshold is crossed and weaker when inflation is already falling quickly.
failure_condition: Reject if triggered observations produce positive 20-day SPX returns in more than 60% of cases, or if the negative effect disappears once CPIAUCSL inflation is slowing.

mechanism_narrative: A 0.3 percentage point or larger rise in UNRATE over three months signals deteriorating labor demand and weaker household income growth, which should depress S&P 500 returns over the next month. The signal fails when unemployment rises alongside a strong increase in PAYEMS.
failure_condition: Reject if, after the trigger, SPX 20-day returns are not negative in at least 55% of events, or if PAYEMS growth offsets the unemployment deterioration and removes the predictive edge.

mechanism_narrative: A simultaneous rise in PAYEMS and decline in UNRATE identifies improving labor demand that supports consumer spending and earnings expectations, producing positive S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days. The mechanism requires both conditions because payroll growth alone can be noisy without a falling unemployment rate.
failure_condition: Reject if the joint condition does not outperform either series alone and if triggered instances fail to generate positive 20-day returns in at least 55% of cases.

mechanism_narrative: A sustained increase in RSAFS indicates stronger retail demand and higher near-term revenue expectations, which should lift S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days. The signal is invalid when sales growth is flat or declining because the consumption impulse is absent.
failure_condition: Reject if rising RSAFS does not correspond to positive SPX 20-day returns in at least 55% of observations, or if rolling 2-year Rank IC remains below 0.03.

mechanism_narrative: When HOUST falls below 900 thousand units, the housing sector signals weakening demand and tighter cyclical activity, which should predict negative S&P 500 returns over the next quarter. The effect is strongest when household demand is also slowing, because housing weakness then reflects broader consumption softness.
failure_condition: Reject if triggered periods fail to show negative 60-day SPX returns in at least 55% of cases, or if housing contractions are consistently followed by positive equity performance.

mechanism_narrative: An acceleration in INDPRO combined with stable CPIAUCSL implies improving real supply without inflation stress, which should support positive S&P 500 returns over the next quarter through margin and earnings relief. The signal fails if output rises only because prices are accelerating faster than production.
failure_condition: Reject if joint improvements in INDPRO and stable CPIAUCSL do not produce positive 60-day SPX returns in at least 55% of cases, or if price inflation rises enough to neutralize the effect.

mechanism_narrative: In a post-shock recovery, rising INDPRO with falling UNRATE signals that idle capacity is being reabsorbed, which should produce positive S&P 500 returns over the next month. This regime-specific effect weakens once unemployment stops improving because the rebound in supply is then already priced in.
failure_condition: Reject if, during post-shock recovery periods, the joint signal does not yield positive 20-day SPX returns in at least 55% of cases or its Rank IC stays below 0.03.
